does: This work introduces a comprehensive account security framework combining phishing-resistant authentication mechanisms with strengthened account recovery procedures and layered protective measures. The system prioritizes prevention of account takeover attacks, the leading cause of unauthorized access to sensitive user data. The implementation emphasizes phishing resistance through cryptographic authentication methods that cannot be compromised by traditional social engineering attacks, while simultaneously improving recovery pathways to prevent lockouts from legitimate users who lose access credentials. The enhanced protections create multiple defense layers that operate redundantly, ensuring that compromise of any single security component does not result in full account breach.
connects: This work extends the long-standing research on authentication security and account protection that has evolved significantly following major industrial phishing attacks and account takeover campaigns. It builds on decades of research into stronger authentication factors, including multi-factor authentication systems, hardware security keys, and cryptographic-based login methods demonstrated effective in prior studies. The recovery mechanisms reference established identity verification literature and anti-account-lockout research that emerged from studying real-world scenarios where legitimate users lost access. The layered defense approach reflects defense-in-depth principles from both classical security research and contemporary incident response studies showing that redundant protections substantially reduce breach likelihood.
enables: This framework enables deployment of enterprise-grade security at scale, potentially reducing account takeover incidents across large user populations. The phishing-resistant authentication methods can serve as reference implementations for broader industry adoption, supporting migration away from password-dependent systems toward more robust authentication standards. The recovery infrastructure creates foundations for exploring seamless security-usability tradeoffs, allowing future work to investigate how strong protections can remain accessible to diverse user populations. The system architecture enables comparative studies of authentication methods in production environments and supports research into detecting compromise attempts before unauthorized access occurs, laying groundwork for predictive account security modeling.
